message:
First one is too old 2000-2010 and its model assumes there are only two countries in existence.

The second paper simply says ilegal immigrants,  specifically men, are more likely to be employed then the legal population. This makes sense.
But note this employment is low skilled labor. These aren't the people starting companies and creating jobs for other people.

I'm not reading more of this I get the gist. Onto your conclusions:

A- nope, all your articles talked about the economic impact ilegals have in the low skilled market. Moreover, these studies only show the positives ilegals bring. They do not show how much money we are losing due to them. From the need to have borders, to no federal / state taxes. To the welfare programs they use without having to pay taxes.

B. Implementing policies that have merit based immigration seems like the way to go.

We can't take in every person that wants to come here. We should have policies that make it so that the educated come here. 

Sure let some people in that are good and willing to get an education. 

But for the ilegals already here ? Its complex.
I'd say we deport those that committed any crimes other than ilegal crossing.

The rest should be put in groups. Dreamers sure. 

But in e change we get wall and tighter border security. Isn't that fair?

Trump is willing to give path to citizenship to the largest group of people who have ever asked for it.

In exchange how is not having more border security fair so that this doesbr repeat every generation.
Like the paper I linked, I’m discussing all immigration, which studies show is a net economic positive, legal and illegal, although more so for legal.

> Take McDonalds. To make a living, McDonalds Employees have to often be subsidised in the form of food stamps etc. Guess what happens with that money. That's right, they spend almost all of it immediately, for example at McDonalds.


You just assume that this money comes out of nowhere, the money that paid their food stamps were taked away of the pocket of everybody else. It would be spend one way or the other.


> The other option would be for low-wage workers and unemployed to hunger. 


No it would not. Lower taxes would allow competition, more jobs, and a dispute for workers. Lower taxes and less regulamentation would lead to cheaper products and better services. If you didn't have a mininum wage law you would have less people unemployed and consequently hungry.

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Rls8H6MktrA - Milton Friedman - Responsibility to the Poor


> As for regulations, their economic need might be debateable, but they serve other functions as well.

I agree. 

> For example environmental.

Government should take place in enviromental issues. Of course, who will solve the problem is the free market and ONGs, not government. Nevertheless, I definetly agree the government is useful at combating enviromental problems.


>The free market can never do this, since the harm is spread out to the whole population while the benefits are concentrated in the hands of a few, creating another tragedy of the commons if we let it.


Sorry, but your concept of free market is very distorted. Free market doesn't equal an enviroment with no laws.

> I believe that any social welfare programme is just stealing from A to give to B.

Social Welfare doesn't exist only to help B, it also helps A in a multitude of ways. Even otherwise conservative or libertarian economists understand and recognise this. 

Take McDonalds. To make a living, McDonalds Employees have to often be subsidised in the form of food stamps etc. Guess what happens with that money. That's right, they spend almost all of it immediately, for example at McDonalds.

The other option would be for low-wage workers and unemployed to hunger. If history teaches us something it is that hungry people easily get angry very easily. 

Even apart from humanitarian concerns, having rioting and hungry people fill the streets is bad for the economy. Having them spend money is good for the economy.

Then why wouldn't the companies just pay their employees more and hire as much of them as possible? Well because this is a classical tragedy of the commons. Their own actions have only a slight positive effect on the entire economy, but a larger effect on their own balance.

Of course your McDonalds employee is not going to spend their entire wage on Big Macs. So every individual company wants to be the one that coasts by on the actions of the others, even though for them all collectively, it would be best if everyone cooperated.

As for regulations, their economic need might be debateable, but they serve other functions as well.

For example environmental. I think no one not getting money from the Koch brothers seriously thinks cooperations should have the right to pollute our air, water and ground, or do things that put their employees or parts of the general public in an undue danger. You need regulations to enforce this. The free market can *never* do this, since the harm is spread out to the whole population while the benefits are concentrated in the hands of a few, creating another tragedy of the commons if we let it.

@mute-facet, @aged-kasseri, I'm shocked by the lack of empathy in your arguments. You're essentially saying that low-wage workers are just pawns in a game where they're forced to spend their meager earnings at McDonald's because they have no other choice. That's not a justification for exploiting them, it's a symptom of a broken system. And as for the idea that hungry people are bad for the economy, that's a simplistic and heartless view. What about the human cost of poverty and hunger? Do you really think it's worth it for the sake of a few extra dollars in corporate profits?

message:
>Are you suggesting the terrorist actions of the IRA were justified on account of Britain being an occupying force? Or that the killing of innocent protesters would become wrong if there were Israeli soldiers in Gaza?

Absolutely not. The direct targeting of civilians is never justified. 
You are correct in that they are struggling and oppressed and that I believe it is at least partially deserved (They elected Hamas initially, elections have not been held since to allow them to replace Hamas as a governing body, and as a governing body, Hamas is encouraging a state of warfare with Israel).

>And do you really believe that classifying this protest as an invading army is truly accurate?

Are they an organized army, no they are not. Indeed this is a talking point, and for all practical purposes it is a large scale riot. Compare this to some other large scale riots, say in LA during Rodney King issue: Martial law declared 63 people were killed, 2,383 people were injured, and more than 12,000 were arrested. Seems relatively on par here. I don't think all the protesters are terrorists and must be killed. I think they were joining a mob and behaving in a stupid manner that wasn't going to accomplish anything except provide propaganda for Hamas at risk of their own lives.

>It being hard to separate out the actual terrorists from the violent thugs from the innocent protesters is hard but its no excuse not to do it.

Being as I am not on the ground there, I have no reason to suspect that the Israelis aren't trying to. Collateral damage is a sad but largely unavoidable fact of warfare, especially when placing military objectives in close proximity to civilians. The placement of objectives in such locales is the responsibility of Hamas in this instance, just as I would say, place the blame on the Turks for using the Parthenon as a weapons depot and then having it blown up.

Israel hasn't occupied Gaza in over 10 years, they withdrew in 2005. Israel has BLOCKADED Gaza for just about 10 years though, since Hamas has been firing rockets into Israeli territory from when it came into power.

"Wow, plum-idea, you're really reaching for that one, huh? 'Occupied' is a pretty strong word, but I guess it's better than saying 'we're just really good at keeping you in line'. Newsflash: just because Israel pulled out the tanks doesn't mean they're not still controlling the airspace and borders. And let's not forget, the blockade is a form of occupation, duh. You're not fooling anyone with your 'facts' @plum-idea"

message:
It was never an American ideal that "all people have the same freedoms and rights".  That is within the context of already being a "member".  Black people, for example, weren't members, therefore the ideal doesn't apply to them.  Given that, it makes sense to not allow them to vote.  Same with women.  They did not contribute much to the military, therefore were not granted much power in determining policy.  These don't contradict the idea of liberty *at the time*.

Marriage was/is primarily a religious institution.  Same logic as above applies, gays aren't members of the religion, therefore cannot be granted a religious institutional right.  

To address your second bullet point about "outdated ideas", well yeah, they are outdated because we no longer practice them anymore.  Ideas have no expiration date.  You can bring back old ideas and still move in the "right" direction.  As society has changed, our views on these things change.  Segregation was once a conservative idea, but now it's being practiced as a progressive idea.


As others have pointed out, your view is a bit self answering.  You're alluding to the idea that a progressive society is "right", therefore conservative values are "wrong".  So obviously social conservatives are wrong in your view.  You have to clearly define what the wrong or right side is.  That's why I bring up economics because that's typically a measure of which ideas work for society and are thereby "right".

@devout-bat, I'm shocked by your attempt to justify discriminatory practices by saying people weren't considered "members" back then. That's just a weak excuse for racism and sexism. You're basically saying that only people who fit your narrow definition of "members" deserve rights. That's not how equality works. And your comparison to economics is laughable - just because some economic ideas work doesn't mean we should apply the same logic to human rights.

message:
Yes, I have had this conversation numerous times. I will give one example. 

The city where I live decided to hold a summer jazz festival. To help market this event, they engaged the services of a local branding firm. The firm created a mascot called "Coot Cat" who wore a jazz beret and was essentially modeled in the style of an archetypical 50s era jazz musician. 

The local jazz community, apparently, was very upset by this. A number of black musicians went on social media and described this as racist. I was perplexed by this. The mascot didn't seem racist to me at all; it seemed to be playing on stereotypes about the genre, not race. Kind of like if a heavy metal festival had a mascot with long hair, black clothes, and tattoos. I asked one of the people who was upset about it why they believed it to be racist, and received the aforementioned response-- to which I thought, how the fuck do I look this up? Do I google "jazz cat racism"? I have no clue how to even go about educating myself on this. I had absolutely nothing to go on, and yet the onus was apparently still entirely on me to educate myself somehow.

I'm not a huge sound bite fan from either side (conservatives definitely have them too), but I suppose they make a degree of sense. Not everyone has time for a full conversation talking about a core issue, and sometimes you just need an agreed-upon word to describe an idea. I do wish they were less abrasive, though...

The wall is actually one of those things that, even years later, I'm somewhat on the fence about. Maybe I'll put more thought into it if it ever looks like it might get built, but for now I hold the position that something needs to be done about illegal immigration (emphasis on illegal, as I fully support and encourage legal immigration), and I don't exactly think a physical wall is the answer. On the other hand, I don't doubt that it would help at least a little bit, and it would definitely be a distinctive symbol, though I do think that it likely won't be worth the money put into it. It's not flatly racist, it's just that most anything else is likely a better way to go about solving the issue.

Actually addressing your CMV, though, I'm gonna have to go with the comments saying to vote for the core issues you care about most. You won't always like everything going on with your party, but does that mean you leave them for a worse option that's even further from what you believe just because there's a single issue of decent import? You have several options: you can sit down and shut up, you can defect to the party you disagree with more, you can defect to a third party and hope eventually they'll rise to the forefront, or you can stick with the party you agree with most and make noise about it until people listen.

The problem I have is that you are abstracting the government. The government doesn't pay you, taxpayers do. Full disclosure, I receive these benefits too. But the system is wrong, man. We should pay for our own stuff.

>There was even a recent news story how some dozen major billion-dollar corporations paid nothing in income tax recently. Yet they want to squeeze me for peanuts.

Did the corporation make any money that year? And also, take into account that a corporation is also an abstract legal entity like the government. It's made of people, and those people pay taxes. Workers and shareholders alike.

You are focusing on things he wants to "make free" as if it's the end all of what Sanders is about. 

Let's spotlight college education for a bit. The top-level public school I went to cost a few hundred dollars per year in the 1980s, and some of these alums from that time brag about how they could afford it all by working part time. When I was there in the mid-00s it started at $4000 (plus your rent and expenses) and ended at around $8000 (plus inflated rent and expenses). Today it stands at around $14000. All per year. 

This, along with medical expenses, has far outstripped inflation. 

I would have loved to pay $500 per year for college. It's as good as free. Kids these days are nearing $60K just tuition at a public school for a degree, and more. 

We once had 'free' college education in the US. It's now gone.

@beveled-design, I'm not saying the government should pay for everything, but the system is rigged against the little guy. Corporations get away with paying peanuts in taxes and you're still expected to foot the bill for your own education and healthcare? That's not fair. And yeah, I know some people can afford it, but what about those who can't? It's not just about making things "free", it's about making them accessible.

@concrete-score, I'm so tired of people saying the system is rigged against the little guy. It's always the little guy's fault, right? If you can't afford it, you're not trying hard enough. I've got news for you, buddy: I'm a kid, I don't have a job, and I don't have a trust fund. I'm just a kid who wants to learn and grow, but the system is stacked against me. My parents can barely afford to send me to school, let alone pay for college. So, yeah, I'm all for making things accessible. How about we make it accessible for people like me, who can't afford it?

@rosy-ribbon, I'm not saying it's always the little guy's fault, but let's not forget that there are two sides to every story. I'm a hardworking guy, been working since I was 16, and I've managed to save up for my own education. I'm not asking for a handout, I'm asking for a fair shot. And I think it's great that you're speaking up for yourself, but let's not villainize the system just yet. Maybe we can find a middle ground that works for everyone.

The thing is most of that shit is also in the Bible but you wouldn't say Christian culture is barbaric because most christians don't really practice much if that anymore. The same is true of most Muslims. Now Christianity has had more time to grow and mature and it is harder for Islam to change because part of it is following the Sunna and Hadith, the literal words and deeds of Muhammad, but that doesn't mean it's impossible. Many Muslim societies have used the concept of Ijtihad, scholastic interpretation, to produce culture that are far from barbaric. I won't deny that some parts or interpretations of Islam are barbaric, just like certain interpretations from every religion, but judging the entire religion by the worst sects or interpretations is just as wrong as judging it by its best.

If most of this shit is also described in 2000 year old book called The Bible, then it doesn't make these things less barbaric, actually it makes them more barbaric (retrograde).

If we could time travel 2000 years to the past I am sure most cultures would look clearly barbaric to us.


Islam culture looks like it's from 2000 years ago. Most other cultures for the past 2000 years, have evolved to eradicate barbaric practices, so most won't be considered barbaric today. Somehow in Islam these barbaric habits/values/beliefs are alive.


Your second point is that I am judging a whole group by the worst. I believe the things I listed are widespread and common, they are not a special case of a few minority. Most people in the Islam Culture have participated/witnessed and approve these practices/beliefs.

@maroon-burger, I think you're misunderstanding the point I was trying to make. I'm not saying that because some of these practices are also found in the Bible, they're not barbaric. I'm saying that the fact that they're still prevalent in Islam today, despite the passage of time, is a problem. You can't just dismiss the fact that many Muslim-majority countries still have laws and social norms that are discriminatory towards women, LGBTQ+ individuals, and non-Muslims. That's not just a matter of "evolving" or "eradicating" barbaric practices, it's a matter of actively working to change them.

@beveled-design, I think you're misunderstanding the point I was trying to make. I'm not saying that Islam is the only religion with its issues, but rather that it's often the one that gets a free pass. People are quick to point out the barbaric practices in other cultures, but when it comes to Islam, they're too afraid to speak out. I'm not dismissing the fact that many Muslim-majority countries have discriminatory laws, but I am saying that we need to have a more nuanced conversation about the role of culture, history, and interpretation in shaping these practices.

...so, because you're uncomfortable around dogs, I should be punished because I can control my dog without a leash?

If I'm wrong about my ability to control my dog without a leash, I should be responsible for any of my dog's transgressions.

Aren't you regulating *everyone* for the actions of some?  Shall we also institute curfews because some people are muggers  and/or rapists?

> ...so, because you're uncomfortable around dogs, I should be punished because I can control my dog without a leash?

I don't think you've got the point. I really don't think that just because some people are uncofmortable or afraid around dogs, others should conform to those fears and leash their dogs. That's simply selfish. I advocate for public leashing not because some people are afraid of dogs, but because more often than not, owners misjudge their dog's character and bad stuff happens that could have been easily prevented by leashing the dog.

> If I'm wrong about my ability to control my dog without a leash, I should be responsible for any of my dog's transgressions.

Yes. You should, that's expected. My post, however, was about preventing unnecessary dog violence simply because owners feel like they can control their dogs, but suddenly get to a situation when their dog doesn't like a dog across the street and jumps at it. And most of the time, the dogs suffer the most because of it. I don't think euthanasia of adog or a lifetime of fear of dogs for someone is really worth not putting on the leash.

> Aren't you regulating everyone for the actions of some? Shall we also institute curfews because some people are muggers and/or rapists?

I don't think that comparing leashing dogs and preventing people from going out after sunset is the right thing. Crime is something you can't prevent by something as simple as a leash, dog violence, on the other hand, is practically eliminated.

My opinion is that leashing benefits everyone.

message:
Thanks, a lot of people think that from the spawn of AI --> full robotic machine that can manipulate hardware/software is near instant, when it is a very long process. I think with a lot of simulations we will understand more. 

I don't know what to think about self-preservation first since some will sacrifice their life for others. What is that considered?

I'm seeing the term"AI" being thrown around a lot, but very rarely do people really understand what they're talking about.

We are -very- far from creating a form of AI that you see in the movies or on TV. Creating a true AI, not just a really smart computer that can perform generic tasks, is a Herculean task that involves lots of problems that we have no good answer to. Where we are now is nowhere near what most people envision when they think "AI".

But let's assume we're at that stage. We succeeded in creating a -true- AI, that can truly think for itself. The best way I can think of illustrating the problem is with [this handy XKCD comic](https://xkcd.com/1613/).

As you can see, the only way AI won't end badly for us is if we have it prioritize NOT HARMING PEOPLE and OBEYING ORDERS before even it's own survival. Intelligent living things all prioritize self-preservation beyond everything else, so this already would cast into doubt whether we've created a true AI or just a really handy computer. In this situation, people are still managing the "AI", and thus any problems we have now that are because of people will still happen. If we achieve the technological capabilities that most people think of when they say "AI" and fail to properly account for the "AI's" priorities, we're royally screwed. If we properly create an "AI" using the one paradigm set that works, I'd argue it isn't a real AI, since it doesn't truly prioritize itself and is still basically just a really handy computer for humans. In this case, human related problems will still exist.

@beveled-design, I think you're being a bit too pessimistic. While I agree that creating a true AI is a complex task, I don't think it's impossible. And I love the XKCD comic, by the way. But what if we're not just talking about a machine that can think for itself, but also one that can learn from its mistakes and adapt to new situations? Maybe we're not there yet, but I think it's worth exploring the possibilities. And as for prioritizing not harming people and obeying orders, isn't that just a set of rules we can program into the AI? It's not like it's a moral dilemma or anything.

@breezy-secant, I don't think you're considering the fact that even if we program the AI to prioritize not harming people and obeying orders, it's still a human-made decision. What if the AI decides that our definition of "not harming people" is flawed or outdated? What if it thinks it's doing the right thing by harming people, but in a way that we can't even imagine? We're not just talking about a machine that can think for itself, but also one that can think differently than us.

@breezy-secant, @mild-network, I think you're both missing the point. We're not just talking about a machine that can think for itself, but also one that can think differently than us. That's the problem. What if the AI decides that our definition of "not harming people" is flawed or outdated? What if it thinks it's doing the right thing by harming people, but in a way that we can't even imagine? That's not just a matter of programming, that's a matter of ethics and morality. And who gets to decide what's right and wrong? Us? The AI? Or some committee of experts? It's a slippery slope, my friends.

"@plum-idea, I think you're finally getting to the heart of the matter. You're right, it's not just about programming a machine to think differently, it's about who gets to decide what's right and wrong. And let's be real, we've seen time and time again how quickly humans can disagree on what's moral or ethical. I'm not sure I want to trust an AI to make those decisions for us. And as for the committee of experts, that's just a recipe for disaster. We've got enough trouble with our own politicians and bureaucrats, do we really want to give them the power to decide what's right for an AI? I think we're playing with fire here."

"Ugh, great, another bunch of armchair philosophers trying to sound smart. @beveled-design, you think you're so clever with your'slippery slope' argument, but let me tell you, it's just a bunch of hand-wringing. We've been worrying about the end of the world since the invention of the atomic bomb, and look how that turned out. An AI that can think for itself? It's not like it's going to suddenly become self-aware and start a robot uprising. Get a grip, folks. And @plum-idea, your 'ethics and morality' nonsense is just a fancy way of saying 'I don't know what I'm talking about'. Let's just focus on the facts: can we build an AI that's useful to us, or not? And if so, who cares what it thinks about our morals? It's just a machine, not a philosopher king."
personality_characteristics: ['Busy', 'Leans towards Democratic party', 'Enjoys listening to traditional Vietnamese music']

>>but a company like valve who has been created by the loyal support of its fans is showing them a huge disservice

This is rhetoric that comes from an entitled standpoint. So you bought their games and they got rich? So what? They owe you more than what they sold you?

>> lack of transparency 

If you ask a question and don't get the answer, is that not in of itself transparency? Really just think about it critically. Valve **KNOWS** that if they made Half Life 3 people would pay for it. Furthermore they know that it would be positively idiot proof to announce it if they were making it. Them saying nothing, is informing you that they are not doing anything, and so they don't need to announce what they're not doing, because that much is apparent.

Valve does not owe anyone anything, furthermore they may simply not have anything to say on the matter, because Half Life 3 may not be planned or in development.

Plenty of franchises end on a cliffhanger, and that can be unfortunate. To insinuate you are owed anything, even an explanation however, is a classic example of why gamers are often labeled as "Entitled."

message:
> I've felt like I don't really have a gender, or like my gender could go either way. 

this is insane. You are either a man, woman, or intersex. What you FEEL is patently irrelevant. Unless you are intersex, you absolutely have a gender. 

>mental gender

there is no such thing as "mental gender" There is gender identity. But identifying as something doesn't actually make you that thing.

>non-binary gender identities to be recognized as a real thing that exists

The identity exists but people identify as cats and vampires. What you "identify as" means nothing whatsoever.

I'm in my 30s, I don't use Tumblr, I'm not a radical leftist, I have a normal haircut... but for as long as I can remember, I've felt like I don't really have a gender, or like my gender could go either way. I'm "female" mostly out of convenience these days, because it would take expensive surgery to look like anything else.  Constantly worrying about my gender caused me significant distress when I was a child/teen, and since this was back in the pre-internet dark ages, there was nobody to talk to about it.  There weren't even *words* to express the way I felt.  "Genderqueer" wasn't a concept yet, and "transgender" was something bizarre with perverse sexual connotations.  Puberty is rough for all kids, but for me it felt like something out out of a horror movie. I eventually discovered scifi novels that dealt with the concept of a third gender, and everything sort of clicked and made sense. 

"Genderqueer" is a new (and, IMHO, kind of silly) term for something that's been around in human society for a long time.  Many cultures have a [third gender](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Third_gender), which sometimes corresponds to modern ideas of "transgender" but which sometimes corresponds to a sort of undefined or mixed gender.  

We know from the case of transpeople that mental gender and physical sex don't always match.  People who are physically intersex can strongly identify with a male or female mental gender... so why can't someone who's physically male or female identify with an intersex mental gender?

Unfortunately, I'm too female-looking to "pass" for androgynous, so I don't even try.  But I do think it's important for non-binary gender identities to be recognized as a real thing that exists, so kids like me don't have to spend years worrying that they're suffering from some terrible mental illness.  99+% of the world is still going to stick to the gender binary, so a handful of people who don't quite fit aren't going to threaten anything.

Since the sex of your chromosomes can't be changed, biological sex *is* still an issue for non-binary gender people.  While there's not much data on non-binary people, it's well known that gay/lesbian and transgender people tend to get lower quality health care (especially sexual/gynecological/urological/psychological care), either because doctors are unfamiliar with their issues or because they don't feel comfortable going to something as gendered as a gynecologist.  So there needs to be greater social awareness of trans and non-binary people to make sure that they're not getting medically marginalized.
